content.text: Understanding how JavaScript stores and scopes values is the foundation of writing correct code. JavaScript has three keywords for declaring variables —
— and they differ in two critical ways: what scope the variable lives in, and whether it can be reassigned or redeclared.
The Three Keywords at a Glance
Scope determines which parts of your code can read a variable. JavaScript has three levels of scope:
— declared outside any function or block; accessible everywhere.
— declared inside a function; only accessible within that function.
(an if-block, loop, etc.); only accessible within those braces.
is function-scoped, meaning it ignores block boundaries. A
block leaks out to the surrounding function:
This leaking behavior is a common source of bugs, which is why
respect block boundaries. A variable declared inside
safer — you always know exactly where a variable exists.
— you cannot point the variable to a different value. But for objects and arrays, the
means "this binding will never point to something else." It does not make objects or arrays frozen. In React, this distinction matters because you can mutate an object declared with
, but mutating React state objects directly breaks state management.
Hoisting is JavaScript's behavior of moving declarations to the top of their scope before any code runs.
are hoisted but not initialized, creating a Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ) where accessing them before the declaration line throws a
is actually safer — it catches bugs where you accidentally use a variable before setting it up.
return is a common source of subtle bugs.
is dangerous in large codebases — you can accidentally overwrite a variable declared elsewhere in the same function without any runtime warning.
bugs involves loops and closures. Because
is function-scoped, all iterations share the same variable:
loops — it creates a fresh binding for each iteration.
In React: Which to Use and When
In React codebases, the convention is straightforward:
for everything that doesn't need to be reassigned: component functions, event handlers, derived values, imported values, hooks.
when you need to reassign: loop counters, variables built up across conditionals.
even though the page changes over time. That's because React state changes work differently —
variable; it triggers a re-render where React calls the component again and
returns a new value for a new
Using let when const would do
if the linter or your own code tells you the variable needs to be reassigned. This makes the code's intent explicit.
Declare variables at the narrowest scope possible.
A variable only needed inside a loop body should be declared inside that loop, not outside. This prevents accidental access from outside code and makes the variable's lifetime obvious.
Understand that React state change ≠ variable reassignment.
— it schedules a re-render where React calls your component again and produces a new constant. This is why state variables are always declared with
, and can be redeclared. Avoid it.
is block-scoped, not hoisted (TDZ), cannot be redeclared. Use it when you need to reassign.
is block-scoped, not hoisted (TDZ), cannot be reassigned or redeclared. Default to this.
const does not make objects/arrays immutable
— only the variable binding is constant; the object's properties can still change.
In React, always use const for state
— changing state re-renders the component with new constants, not reassigned variables.
